title: Agentic Design at Scale
subtitle: Using AI-coded prototypes to collapse the design-to-production pipeline

sections.headline: Agentic Design at Scale
sections.subtext: Designing in prototypes, not mockups. AI agents generate the code. Engineering teams ship the output directly.

images.caption: From design intent to shipped code in a single loop

sections.headline: The Traditional Gap
sections.content:
Most design workflows follow this pattern:

1. Designer creates mockups in Figma
2. Designer writes specs (pixel-perfect measurements, interaction states)
3. Engineering team reads specs, estimates time
4. Engineering rebuilds what's in the mockup
5. QA tests, finds gaps (interactions not fully specified)
6. Designer and engineer collaborate to close gaps
7. Final code ships

This process has built-in delay and ambiguity. Even with the best specs, engineering has to make hundreds of micro-decisions about behavior that specs don't capture.

sections.quote: What if instead of writing specs, I just showed the prototype *code* and the engineering team decided whether to ship it as-is, modify, or rebuild it cleanly?
sections.context: The insight that changed our process

sections.headline: The AI-First Approach
sections.content:
At Bibel TV, we're experimenting with a different workflow:

### Step 1: Design first

I design in Figma as usual, but think in interactions and behaviors, not static mockups.

### Step 2: Prototype in code

Rather than writing specs, I describe the intent to Claude Code: "Build a mobile app screen where users can browse spiritual content, filter by category, save to favorites, and see recently watched." Claude generates a React Native (or Swift, or Kotlin) prototype.

### Step 3: Engineer reviews and decides

The engineering team sees working code. They evaluate: ship as-is? Modify? Rebuild cleanly?

Why this works: the prototype is often good enough for specific platforms. For shared logic, the team uses it as a reference. For UI-specific platforms, they rebuild with confidence that the interaction model is validated.

### Step 4: Ship

Code ships, user feedback guides iteration.

steps.label: Interaction Design
steps.description: I design interactions in Figma, annotate key behaviors (swipes, gestures, state changes). Focus on user intent, not pixel perfection.

steps.label: Prototype Generation
steps.description: I brief Claude Code: "Here's what I designed. Build this as a functioning prototype." Claude generates working code (React, React Native, Swift, or Kotlin depending on target).

steps.label: Prototype Testing
steps.description: I test the prototype on device, verify interactions match intent. Iterate with Claude if behavior is off.

steps.label: Engineering Review
steps.description: Engineering team reviews the prototype code. Evaluates architecture, performance, whether it's production-ready.

image.caption: Code prototype ready for engineering evaluation

steps.label: Ship or Rebuild Decision
steps.description: Team decides: Ship the prototype as-is (if good enough), modify it, or rebuild using the prototype as a spec.

sections.headline: Real Example: New Mobile App Redesign
sections.content:
Our current project is a complete Bibel TV app redesign for iOS and Android. Using agentic design:

### The traditional approach

Design mockups, write a 50-page spec, engineer builds from scratch, QA finds gaps, iterate. Four to six weeks.

### Our approach

Design interactions in Figma. I brief Claude: "Build a React Native app with these screens, this navigation structure, these animations." Claude generates a 95%-complete prototype. Engineering team ships ~70% of it as-is, rebuilds 30% for performance/architecture.

Result: first version ships in 2 weeks, with higher fidelity interactions than specs could have captured.

The win: engineering team is no longer blindfolded by incomplete specs. They see actual working behavior and make informed decisions about what to keep or rebuild.

sections.headline: Key Insights
sections.content:
### AI doesn't replace design thinking

The prototype code is only as good as the intent I give it. Bad brief, bad code. But a good brief plus AI beats specs-then-rebuild every time.

### Engineering makes the call

I don't force-ship AI-generated code. The team evaluates, modifies, or rebuilds. This preserves engineering autonomy and code quality.

### Interaction fidelity matters

Where AI-generated code shines is capturing interactions — animations, gestures, state transitions — that specs typically under-specify. Shipping these interactions directly is a win.

### Platform-specific code still gets rebuilt

The React prototype works great as a reference for iOS and Android teams, but they often rebuild platform-native for better performance and UX consistency. That's fine — the prototype still saved time and validated the interaction model.

sections.headline: Results So Far

metrics.label: Spec-to-Code Ambiguity
metrics.after: Typical: 5-10 clarifications (most questions answered by seeing working code)

metrics.label: Prototype-to-Ship Rate
metrics.value: ~70% of AI-generated code ships directly or with minor modifications

metrics.label: Engineering Team Satisfaction
metrics.value: High — they prefer working from code prototypes vs. specs

sections.narrative: The biggest win is eliminated ambiguity. When engineering sees working code instead of a mockup, they ask better questions and make faster decisions.

images.caption: From design intent to shipped code, collapsed timeline

sections.headline: What I'm Learning
sections.content:
Five years ago, I would've said "designers don't code prototypes because spec communication is efficient." That was wrong. Specs are *lossy*. They can't capture all the micro-decisions a designer makes.

What we're learning: AI-generated prototypes are efficient *and* high-fidelity. They preserve intent better than specs. They let engineering make informed decisions instead of guessing from drawings.

This might be the biggest shift in design-engineering collaboration since design systems.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If you're stuck in the traditional design-handoff-to-engineering loop, agentic prototyping is the escape route. I'm proving it works at Bibel TV with real products, real deadlines, and real engineering teams. The prototype doesn't have to be perfect — it just has to be better than a spec at communicating intent.

executive.summary: Redesigned the design-to-engineering workflow using AI-generated prototypes. Result: 2x faster design-to-prototype cycle, 70% of code ships directly.
executive.angle: Collapsing the design-engineering loop with agentic code

in-depth.summary: Deep dive into agentic design: how we use Claude Code to generate prototypes, how engineering teams evaluate them, and what this means for the future of design handoff.
in-depth.angle: Prototypes as specs: a working alternative to pixel-perfect mockups
